testTitle: IELTS Academic Level 5.0 - Globalization and Cultural Exchange

sections.title: Listening
sections.description: 40 questions • Approximately 30 minutes • You will hear each recording only once

parts.title: Part 1: Conversation about studying abroad experiences
parts.instructions: Listen to the conversation and answer questions 1-10.

script.narrator:
Good morning. This is the International English Language Testing System, IELTS. You will hear a number of different recordings and you will have to answer questions on what you hear. There will be time for you to read the instructions and questions and you will have a chance to check your work. All the recordings will be played once only. The test is in four parts. At the end of the test you will be given ten minutes to transfer your answers to an answer sheet.

Now turn to Part 1. You will hear a conversation between an international student advisor and a prospective student about studying abroad. Look at questions 1 to 10. You now have some time to look at the questions.

[Pause]

Now listen carefully and answer questions 1 to 10.

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: Good morning, welcome to the International Student Office. How can I help you today?

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: Good morning. I'm considering applying to study here next semester and I have some questions about the application process and student life.

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: Of course. Let me start by explaining that our application deadline for the September intake is June 30th. You'll need to submit your academic transcripts, English language test results, and two reference letters.

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: What English test scores do you accept? I have IELTS but I'm wondering if I need to retake it.

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: We require an overall band score of 6.0 with no section below 5.5. If your scores meet this requirement, they're valid for two years from the test date.

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: That's good to know. My current score is 5.5 overall. About accommodation - what options are available for international students?

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: We have university halls of residence which cost between £120 and £180 per week, or you can choose private accommodation. The halls include utilities and internet, while private rentals usually don't.

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: And what about part-time work? I'd like to get some work experience while studying.

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: International students can work up to 20 hours per week during term time. We have a careers service that helps students find part-time positions, but I should warn you that competition can be quite high.

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: Thank you, that's very helpful. One last question - what support services are available for students who might struggle with cultural adjustment?

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: We have a comprehensive support system including a mentoring program, cultural orientation workshops, and counseling services. Many students find our weekly international student coffee mornings particularly helpful for making friends.

questions.questionText: What is the application deadline for September intake?
questions.translation: Hạn nộp đơn cho kỳ nhập học tháng 9 là khi nào?

questions.questionText: How many reference letters are required?
questions.translation: Cần bao nhiêu thư giới thiệu?

questions.questionText: What IELTS score is required overall?
questions.translation: Điểm IELTS tổng thể yêu cầu là bao nhiêu?

questions.questionText: How long are IELTS scores valid?
questions.translation: Điểm IELTS có hiệu lực trong bao lâu?

questions.questionText: What is the weekly cost of university halls?
questions.translation: Chi phí hàng tuần cho ký túc xá đại học là bao nhiêu?

questions.questionText: What is included in halls of residence?
questions.translation: Những gì được bao gồm trong ký túc xá?

questions.questionText: How many hours can international students work weekly?
questions.translation: Sinh viên quốc tế có thể làm việc bao nhiêu giờ mỗi tuần?

questions.questionText: What does the careers service help with?
questions.translation: Dịch vụ nghề nghiệp giúp đỡ về việc gì?

questions.questionText: What support is mentioned for cultural adjustment?
questions.translation: Hỗ trợ nào được đề cập cho việc thích nghi văn hóa?

questions.questionText: When are the international student coffee mornings?
questions.translation: Buổi sáng cà phê cho sinh viên quốc tế diễn ra khi nào?

parts.title: Part 2: Monologue about cultural adaptation challenges
parts.instructions: Listen to the talk and answer questions 11-20.

script.narrator:
Now turn to Part 2. You will hear a talk by a cultural adaptation advisor. Look at questions 11 to 20. You now have some time to look at the questions.

[Pause]

Now listen carefully and answer questions 11 to 20.

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: Good afternoon everyone. Today I'd like to discuss the common challenges international students face when adapting to life in the UK and some strategies to overcome them. The first few months can be particularly difficult as you adjust to a new educational system, different social norms, and sometimes a completely unfamiliar climate. Many students experience what we call 'culture shock' during this period. This typically involves four stages: the initial honeymoon phase where everything seems exciting, followed by frustration as differences become more apparent, then gradual adjustment, and finally acceptance and adaptation. One of the biggest challenges is the British education system, which often emphasizes independent study and critical thinking rather than memorization. Students from educational systems that focus on rote learning may find this transition challenging. Socially, British people can seem reserved at first. They value personal space and may not immediately invite you into their social circles. However, joining student clubs and participating in university events can help you make friends. The weather is another common challenge, especially for students from warmer climates. The lack of sunlight during winter months can affect your mood, so it's important to get outside when possible and consider vitamin D supplements. Food is another area of adjustment. While British cuisine may be different from what you're used to, most cities have international supermarkets where you can find ingredients from home. Finally, don't hesitate to use the support services available at the university. We're here to help you succeed in your studies and enjoy your time in the UK.

questions.questionText: What is the main topic of the talk?
questions.translation: Chủ đề chính của bài nói là gì?

questions.questionText: How many stages of culture shock are mentioned?
questions.translation: Có bao nhiêu giai đoạn sốc văn hóa được đề cập?

questions.questionText: What comes after the honeymoon phase?
questions.translation: Điều gì đến sau giai đoạn trăng mật?

questions.questionText: What does the British education system emphasize?
questions.translation: Hệ thống giáo dục Anh nhấn mạnh điều gì?

questions.questionText: What might British people value in social situations?
questions.translation: Người Anh có thể coi trọng điều gì trong các tình huống xã hội?

questions.questionText: What is suggested for making friends?
questions.translation: Điều gì được đề xuất để kết bạn?

questions.questionText: What weather challenge is mentioned?
questions.translation: Thách thức thời tiết nào được đề cập?

questions.questionText: What supplement is recommended?
questions.translation: Chất bổ sung nào được đề xuất?

questions.questionText: Where can students find ingredients from home?
questions.translation: Sinh viên có thể tìm nguyên liệu từ quê hương ở đâu?

questions.questionText: What final advice is given?
questions.translation: Lời khuyên cuối cùng là gì?

parts.title: Part 3: Discussion about global food trends
parts.instructions: Listen to the discussion and answer questions 21-30.

script.narrator:
Now turn to Part 3. You will hear a discussion between a nutrition expert and a food journalist about global food trends. Look at questions 21 to 30. You now have some time to look at the questions.

[Pause]

Now listen carefully and answer questions 21 to 30.

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: I've been researching how globalization has affected food cultures worldwide. It's fascinating to see how cuisines have evolved through cross-cultural exchange.

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: Absolutely. What we're seeing is a fascinating blend of traditional cooking methods with international ingredients. For example, the popularity of Korean kimchi in Western countries or the adaptation of pizza with local toppings in different cultures.

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: Yes, and social media has accelerated this process dramatically. Platforms like Instagram and TikTok have made exotic foods accessible and desirable to global audiences almost instantly.

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: That's true, but there are also concerns about cultural appropriation versus appreciation. When foods from marginalized communities become trendy without proper acknowledgment or benefit to those communities, it can be problematic.

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: An important point. On a more positive note, I've noticed increased interest in plant-based diets globally. This seems to be driven by health concerns, environmental awareness, and animal welfare considerations.

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: Definitely. The rise of veganism and flexitarian diets represents a significant shift. Interestingly, many traditional cuisines from Asian and Middle Eastern cultures have always included substantial plant-based components, so in some ways, we're rediscovering ancient wisdom.

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: Another trend I've observed is the growing demand for authentic ethnic restaurants rather than Westernized versions. Consumers today are more educated about different cuisines and seek genuine experiences.

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: That connects to the slow food movement and interest in food provenance. People want to know where their food comes from, how it was produced, and the stories behind traditional dishes.

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: However, there's also the concerning trend of dietary homogenization. As global fast-food chains expand, we risk losing unique local food traditions and biodiversity.

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: A valid concern. That's why food education and supporting local producers are so crucial. Ultimately, the ideal scenario is a balance between enjoying global food diversity while preserving culinary heritage.

questions.questionText: What is the main topic of discussion?
questions.translation: Chủ đề thảo luận chính là gì?

questions.questionText: What example is given of cross-cultural food exchange?
options: D] Korean kimchi in the West
questions.translation: Ví dụ nào được đưa ra về trao đổi ẩm thực đa văn hóa?

questions.questionText: What has accelerated food trends according to the speakers?
questions.translation: Điều gì đã đẩy nhanh xu hướng ẩm thực theo các diễn giả?

questions.questionText: What concern is mentioned about food trends?
questions.translation: Mối quan tâm nào được đề cập về xu hướng ẩm thực?

questions.questionText: What diet trend is discussed?
questions.translation: Xu hướng ăn kiêng nào được thảo luận?

questions.questionText: What drives interest in plant-based diets?
questions.translation: Điều gì thúc đẩy sự quan tâm đến chế độ ăn thực vật?

questions.questionText: What type of restaurants are consumers seeking?
questions.translation: Người tiêu dùng đang tìm kiếm loại nhà hàng nào?

questions.questionText: What movement is connected to food provenance?
questions.translation: Phong trào nào được kết nối với nguồn gốc thực phẩm?

questions.questionText: What concerning trend is mentioned?
questions.translation: Xu hướng đáng lo ngại nào được đề cập?

questions.questionText: What is described as the ideal scenario?
options: D] Balance of global diversity and local heritage
questions.translation: Điều gì được mô tả là kịch bản lý tưởng?

parts.title: Part 4: Lecture on the impact of English as a global language
parts.instructions: Listen to the lecture and answer questions 31-40.

script.narrator:
Now turn to Part 4. You will hear a lecture on the impact of English as a global language. Look at questions 31 to 40. You now have some time to look at the questions.

[Pause]

Now listen carefully and answer questions 31 to 40.

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: Today we'll examine the phenomenon of English as a global language and its far-reaching implications. English has become the dominant international language of business, science, technology, and diplomacy. This status can be traced back to the British Empire's historical expansion and has been maintained through American economic and cultural influence in the 20th and 21st centuries. The rise of English as a global language has created both opportunities and challenges. On the positive side, it facilitates international communication and cooperation. Scientists from different countries can collaborate effectively, businesses can operate across borders, and travelers can navigate foreign countries more easily. However, there are also significant concerns. The dominance of English can marginalize other languages and contribute to language extinction. Some linguists estimate that up to 90% of the world's languages could disappear within the next century. Furthermore, the spread of English creates inequalities between those who have access to English education and those who don't. In many countries, proficiency in English has become a marker of social class and educational privilege. Another interesting development is the emergence of various English dialects and 'World Englishes' - localized versions of English that incorporate vocabulary and grammatical structures from local languages. These include Indian English, Singaporean English, and Nigerian English, among others. Rather than viewing these as incorrect versions of English, many linguists now recognize them as legitimate varieties that reflect cultural identity. As we move forward, the key question is whether English will continue to dominate globally or whether we'll see a more multilingual approach to international communication, perhaps with technological assistance like real-time translation becoming more sophisticated.

questions.questionText: What is the main topic of the lecture?
options: A) English as a global language
questions.translation: Chủ đề chính của bài giảng là gì?

questions.questionText: What maintained English's global status in the 20th century?
questions.translation: Điều gì duy trì vị thế toàn cầu của tiếng Anh trong thế kỷ 20?

questions.questionText: What positive aspect of global English is mentioned?
questions.translation: Khía cạnh tích cực nào của tiếng Anh toàn cầu được đề cập?

questions.questionText: What concern is raised about language diversity?
questions.translation: Mối quan tâm nào được nêu lên về sự đa dạng ngôn ngữ?

questions.questionText: What percentage of languages might disappear?
questions.translation: Bao nhiêu phần trăm ngôn ngữ có thể biến mất?

questions.questionText: What has English proficiency become in many countries?
questions.translation: Trình độ tiếng Anh đã trở thành điều gì ở nhiều quốc gia?

questions.questionText: What are 'World Englishes'?
questions.translation: 'World Englishes' là gì?

questions.questionText: What examples of World Englishes are given?
questions.translation: Những ví dụ nào về World Englishes được đưa ra?

questions.questionText: How do linguists view these English varieties?
questions.translation: Các nhà ngôn ngữ học xem các biến thể tiếng Anh này như thế nào?

questions.questionText: What technological development might affect English's dominance?
questions.translation: Phát triển công nghệ nào có thể ảnh hưởng đến sự thống trị của tiếng Anh?

passages.title: Passage 1: The Rise of International Student Mobility
passages.content:
In recent decades, there has been a significant increase in the number of students choosing to pursue higher education in foreign countries. This trend, known as international student mobility, has been driven by various factors including globalization, improved transportation, and the growing recognition of the value of international experience in the job market.

The United States, United Kingdom, Australia, and Canada remain the most popular destinations for international students. These countries offer high-quality education, English-language instruction, and post-study work opportunities that appeal to students worldwide. However, Asian countries like China, Singapore, and Malaysia are increasingly attracting international students with their rapidly improving educational institutions and more affordable tuition fees.

Studying abroad offers numerous benefits to students. It enhances language skills, develops cross-cultural understanding, and builds independence. Employers often value the adaptability and global perspective that international education fosters. Additionally, many students form lifelong friendships and professional networks that span different countries.

Despite these advantages, international students face several challenges. Cultural adjustment can be difficult, with many experiencing homesickness and culture shock. Financial pressures are also significant, as international tuition fees are typically much higher than those for domestic students. Furthermore, navigating different educational systems and teaching styles requires adaptability.

Universities have responded to the growth in international student numbers by developing support services specifically designed to help these students adjust to their new environment. These include orientation programs, academic support, counseling services, and social events that facilitate cultural exchange.

Looking forward, international student mobility is expected to continue growing, though possibly at a slower pace due to factors such as changing immigration policies, economic fluctuations, and the increased availability of high-quality educational options in students' home countries.

questions.questionText: What is the main topic of the passage?
Which countries are mentioned as most popular for international students?
What is NOT mentioned as a benefit of studying abroad?
What challenges do international students face?
options: D] Cultural adjustment and financial pressures

questions.questionText: How have universities responded to international students?
What might slow the growth of student mobility?
Why are Asian countries attracting more students?
options: D] Improving education and affordable fees

questions.questionText: What do employers value in internationally educated students?
What services do universities provide?
What is the expected future trend?

passages.title: Passage 2: The Globalization of Food Culture
passages.content:
The globalization of food culture represents one of the most visible aspects of cultural exchange in the modern world. As people travel more frequently and migration patterns shift, culinary traditions cross borders and evolve in new contexts. This phenomenon has transformed eating habits worldwide and created what some scholars call 'food globalization'.

Historical trade routes like the Silk Road facilitated early food globalization, introducing spices, fruits, and cooking techniques across continents. In the contemporary era, this process has accelerated dramatically due to advances in transportation, refrigeration, and communication technology. Supermarkets in London stock mangoes from India, avocados from Mexico, and salmon from Norway, while restaurants in Tokyo serve pizza, pasta, and hamburgers alongside traditional Japanese cuisine.

The fast-food industry has been a major driver of food globalization. American chains like McDonald's, KFC, and Starbucks have established presence in countless countries, often adapting their menus to local tastes. For example, McDonald's offers vegetarian options in India and rice dishes in Southeast Asia. While criticized for contributing to dietary homogenization and health problems, these chains have also created employment opportunities and introduced new business models.

Simultaneously, there has been growing interest in authentic ethnic cuisines and traditional cooking methods. Food television programs, cooking blogs, and social media have educated consumers about different culinary traditions. This has led to increased demand for genuine ethnic restaurants rather than Westernized versions of foreign foods.

The slow food movement, which originated in Italy as a response to fast food, emphasizes local ingredients, traditional cooking methods, and sustainable production. This movement has gained international followers who value food quality, environmental responsibility, and cultural preservation.

Food globalization raises important questions about cultural appropriation versus appreciation. When foods from marginalized communities become trendy without proper acknowledgment or benefit to those communities, it can reinforce economic and social inequalities. However, when approached respectfully, culinary exchange can promote cross-cultural understanding and appreciation.

As food cultures continue to globalize, the challenge lies in balancing the enjoyment of global food diversity with the preservation of local culinary heritage and sustainable food practices.

questions.questionText: What is the main topic of the passage?
What historical example of food exchange is mentioned?
What has accelerated food globalization?
How have fast-food chains adapted to local markets?
What has increased demand for authentic ethnic restaurants?
Where did the slow food movement originate?
What does the slow food movement emphasize?
What concern is raised about food trends?
What positive outcome can culinary exchange have?
What is the main challenge mentioned?
options: D] Balancing global diversity and local heritage

passages.title: Passage 3: The Impact of Global Tourism on Local Communities
passages.content:
Global tourism has become one of the world's largest industries, with international tourist arrivals exceeding 1.4 billion annually before the COVID-19 pandemic. While tourism generates significant economic benefits, its impact on local communities and environments is complex and multifaceted.

Economically, tourism creates jobs and generates income for local businesses. In many developing countries, tourism represents a major source of foreign exchange and government revenue. However, the economic benefits are not always distributed evenly. Large international hotel chains often capture a substantial portion of tourism revenue, while local workers may receive low wages and seasonal employment.

Culturally, tourism can both preserve and commodify local traditions. On one hand, the interest of tourists in authentic cultural experiences can encourage communities to maintain traditional practices, crafts, and festivals that might otherwise disappear. On the other hand, cultural performances and artifacts may be modified to suit tourist expectations, potentially losing their original meaning and significance.

Environmentally, tourism poses significant challenges. The construction of hotels, resorts, and infrastructure can damage natural habitats and ecosystems. Increased waste generation, water consumption, and carbon emissions from transportation contribute to environmental degradation. However, tourism can also provide economic incentives for conservation, as pristine natural environments often attract visitors.

Overtourism has emerged as a serious problem in many popular destinations. Cities like Venice, Barcelona, and Dubrovnik have experienced protests from residents concerned about overcrowding, rising living costs, and the transformation of their communities into what some critics call 'theme parks' for tourists.

Sustainable tourism approaches seek to maximize benefits while minimizing negative impacts. These include ecotourism, community-based tourism, and responsible travel practices. Ecotourism emphasizes nature conservation and environmental education, while community-based tourism ensures that local communities have control over and benefit from tourism development.

The COVID-19 pandemic caused an unprecedented collapse in global tourism, providing an opportunity to reconsider tourism models. Some destinations used this pause to develop more sustainable tourism strategies, addressing issues of carrying capacity, resident quality of life, and environmental protection.

As global tourism recovers, the industry faces the challenge of rebuilding in a way that balances economic benefits with social, cultural, and environmental sustainability. This requires cooperation between governments, tourism businesses, local communities, and tourists themselves.

questions.questionText: What is the main topic of the passage?
What economic benefit does tourism provide?
What problem is mentioned about economic benefits?
How can tourism affect local cultures?
options: D] Both preserve and commodify traditions

questions.questionText: What environmental challenges does tourism create?
What is 'overtourism'?
What do sustainable tourism approaches aim to do?
What opportunity did the COVID-19 pandemic provide?
What does ecotourism emphasize?
What is needed for sustainable tourism development?

tasks.title: Writing Task 1
tasks.instructions: You should spend about 20 minutes on this task. The chart below shows the number of international students studying in English-speaking countries from 2000 to 2020. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant. Write at least 150 words.
tasks.description: You will be presented with a chart showing international student numbers and asked to describe the information in your own words.

sampleData.title: International Students in English-Speaking Countries (2000-2020)
sampleData.description: Line chart showing growth in international student numbers in four English-speaking countries over two decades

tasks.title: Writing Task 2
tasks.instructions: You should spend about 40 minutes on this task. Some people believe that the increasing globalization of culture through media and the internet has negative effects on local cultures and traditions. Others argue that this cultural exchange is beneficial. Discuss both these views and give your own opinion. Write at least 250 words.
tasks.description: You will be presented with contrasting views on cultural globalization and asked to discuss them.
keyPoints: Arguments about negative effects on local cultures
Arguments about benefits of cultural exchange

parts.title: Part 1: Introduction and Interview
parts.instructions: The examiner will ask you questions about yourself and familiar topics.
sampleQuestions: Can you tell me about your hometown?
What do you like about your job/studies?
How do you usually spend your free time?
Have you traveled to other countries?
What kind of music do you enjoy listening to?

parts.title: Part 2: Individual Long Turn
parts.instructions: You will be given a topic and have one minute to prepare before speaking for 1-2 minutes.

taskCard.topic: Describe an interesting tradition in your culture.

parts.title: Part 3: Two-way Discussion
parts.instructions: The examiner will ask further questions connected to the topic in Part 2.
sampleQuestions: How are traditional celebrations changing in your country?
Why is it important to preserve cultural traditions?
How does globalization affect local cultures?
Do you think young people are less interested in traditions than older generations?
How can traditional customs be kept alive in modern society?

metadata.author: IELTS Academic Testing System
metadata.notes: IELTS Academic Level 5.0 test with realistic topics and balanced difficulty. This test reflects actual IELTS format and content suitable for intermediate English learners aiming for band 5.0.
